sat_relevance: Relevant when sweetness, concentration, texture or botrytised aroma is explained by noble rot.

steps.label: cause
steps.text: Beneficial Botrytis cinerea infects ripe grapes when humid or misty periods are followed by warm, dry conditions.

steps.label: mechanism
steps.text: The fungus perforates the grape skin, allowing water to evaporate during dry periods and concentrating sugars, remaining acids, flavour compounds and glycerol precursors in the shrinking berry. Botrytis also metabolises some grape acids, so net acidity is lower than simple concentration alone would produce.

steps.label: effect
steps.text: The fruit develops greater sugar, remaining-acid and flavour concentration, a rich texture and characteristic noble-rot aromas suitable for intensely sweet wines, with acidity lower than the concentration effect alone would predict.

steps.label: exam_formulation
steps.text: Noble rot increases concentration because perforated grape skins permit water loss, while Botrytis acid metabolism means net acidity remains lower than simple concentration alone would predict.

_meta.source_note: Inferred formative model - Phase P.3 Botrytis candidate expansion. Not from official WSET mark scheme.
